result.diagram_code:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Attacker
    participant Server
    participant Initiator
    participant Responder

    alt Leak of secretA
        Attacker->>Server: request certs for (B,B)
        Server-->>Attacker: sign((B,spkB),skS), sign((B,pkB),skS)
        Attacker->>Initiator: xA = B
        Attacker->>Initiator: cert1=sign((B,spkB),skS), cert2=sign((B,pkB),skS)
        Initiator->>Initiator: verify certs, create fresh k
        Initiator-->>Attacker: sign((B,B,k),sskB)
        Attacker->>Attacker: getmess(...) -> (B,B,k), extract k
        Initiator-->>Attacker: sencrypt(secretA,k)
        Attacker->>Attacker: sdecrypt(...,k) = secretA
    end

    alt Leak of secretB
        Attacker->>Server: request certs for (A,B)
        Server-->>Attacker: sign((A,spkA),skS), sign((B,pkB),skS)
        Attacker->>Server: request certs for (B,B)
        Server-->>Attacker: sign((B,spkB),skS), sign((B,pkB),skS)
        Attacker->>Initiator: xA = A
        Attacker->>Initiator: cert1=sign((A,spkA),skS), cert2=sign((B,spkB),skS)
        Initiator->>Initiator: verify cert1, parse cert2, create fresh k
        Initiator-->>Attacker: sign((A,B,k),sskA)
        Attacker->>Attacker: getmess(...) -> (A,B,k), extract k
        Attacker->>Responder: xB = B
        Attacker->>Responder: cert1=sign((A,spkA),skS), cert2=sign((B,pkB),skS), km=sign((A,B,k),sskA)
        Responder->>Responder: verify km with pk1 from cert1
        Responder-->>Attacker: sencrypt(secretB,k)
        Attacker->>Attacker: sdecrypt(...,k) = secretB
    end
result.explanation: The trace shows that the session key k is sent only inside a signature, and in this model signatures are publicly readable via getmess(sign(m,sk)) = m. So the attacker can recover k from sign((A,B,k),sskA) or sign((B,B,k),sskB), then decrypt sencrypt(secretA,k); the same recovered k can also be replayed to the responder to obtain and decrypt sencrypt(secretB,k).
result.patch_suggestion: Use the recipient encryption key from cert2 to protect the signed session-key message. In the initiator, replace out(c, (cert1, cert2, sign((xA,h2,k), sskA))) with out(c, (cert1, cert2, encrypt(sign((xA,h2,k), sskA), pk2))). In the responder, decrypt first and then verify: let ks = decrypt(km, skB) in let (=h1, =xB, k:key) = checksign(ks, pk1) in ... . This is the minimal change that prevents the attacker from learning k from the clear signature.
result.notes: The rebinding warnings are present, but the secrecy break shown by the trace is directly due to k being exposed in a signed plaintext token. The trace also shows the attacker can choose xA/xB to start roles as A or B.
